raw_text.question:
Imagine you're analyzing a series of signals from a remote sensor that detects environmental changes. The sensor can either signal an alert for a volcanic eruption (which we'll call 'eruption') or remain normal ('normal'). The probability of an eruption on any given day is 0.6, and the probability of a normal day is 0.4. The condition on day $k$ depends on the condition of day $k-1$, with the probability of the condition remaining the same (eruption to eruption or normal to normal) being 0.55. A research station nearby monitors these signals and has a system that reacts to these environmental changes. The system has a 60% chance of activating (sending an alert signal) on an eruption day and a 40% chance of activating on a normal day, i.e., P(alert $	ext{ signal }$ $	ext{ given }$ eruption) = 0.6 and P(alert $	ext{ signal }$ $	ext{ given }$ normal) = 0.4. Over three consecutive days, the system (1) sent an alert signal, (2) did not send an alert signal, and (3) sent an alert signal again. Based on this pattern of signals, what is the most likely sequence of environmental conditions (eruption or normal) over these three days? Return the answer as a list of binary values, where 1 represents an eruption and 0 represents a normal condition.

Retrieved documents:
[Document 1]
\section{Finite Group has Composition Series}
Tags: Normal Series

\begin{theorem}
Let $G$ be a finite group.
Then $G$ has a composition series.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Let $G$ be a finite group whose identity is $e$.
Either $G$ has a proper non-trivial normal subgroup or it does not.
If not, then:
:$\set e \lhd G$
is the composition series for $G$.
Otherwise, $G$ has one or more proper non-trivial normal subgroup.
Of these, one or more will have a maximum order.
Select one of these and call it $G_1$.
Again, either $G_1$ has a proper non-trivial normal subgroup or it does not.
If not, then:
:$\set e \lhd G_1 \lhd G$
is a composition series for $G$.
By the Jordan-Hölder Theorem, there can be no other composition series which is longer.
As $G_1$ is a proper subgroup of $G$:
:$\order {G_1} < \order G$
where $\order G$ denotes the order of $G$.
Again, if $G_1$ has one or more proper non-trivial normal subgroup, one or more will have a maximum order.
Select one of these and call it $G_2$.
Thus we form a normal series:
:$\set e \lhd G_2 \lhd G_1 \lhd G$
The process can be repeated, and at each stage a normal subgroup is added to the series of a smaller 
order than the previous one.
This process can not continue infinitely.
Eventually a $G_n$ will be encountered which has no proper non-trivial normal subgroup.
Thus a composition series:
:$\set e \lhd G_n \cdots \lhd G_2 \lhd G_1 \lhd G$
will be the result.
{{qed}}
{{proofread}}
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Definition:Valid Time Indication]
Consider an analogue clock with an hour hand $H$ and a minute hand $M$.
A '''valid time indication''' is an arrangement of $H$ and $M$ which indicates a specific time of day (either a.m. or p.m.).
{{handwaving|It is not at all clear what it means to "indicate a specific time of day" since we have not defined how a clock works. I can imagine we don't want to go that far because it would be beside the point, but then we should explain that it is different from a random orientation of H and M which does not correspond to normal operation of a clock.}}
Category:Definitions/Clocks
\end{definition}

[Document 3]
\section{Number of Friday 13ths in a Year}
Tags: Calendars

\begin{theorem}
In any given year, there are between $1$ and $3$ (inclusive) months in which the $13$th falls on a Friday.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
The day of the week on which the $13$th falls is directy dependent upon the day of the week that the $1$st of the month falls.
From Months that Start on the Same Day of the Week, the months can be grouped into equivalence classes according to which day the month starts:
For a non-leap year, the set of equivalence classes is:
:$\set {\set {\text {January}, \text {October} }, \set {\text {February}, \text {March}, \text {November} }, \set {\text {April}, \text {July} }, \set {\text {May} }, \set {\text {June} }, \set {\text {August} }, \set {\text {September}, \text {December} } }$
For a leap year, the set of equivalence classes is:
:$\set {\set {\text {January}, \text {April}, \text {July} }, \set {\text {February}, \text {August} }, \set {\text {March}, \text {November} }, \set {\text {May} }, \set {\text {June} }, \set {\text {September}, \text {December} }, \set {\text {October} } }$
It can be seen that none of these equivalence classes has fewer than $1$ day, and none has more than $3$.
Hence the result.
{{qed}}
\end{proof}



[Document 4]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Coin/Coin-Tossing]
'''Coin-tossing''' is a technique to select randomly one of two options. The usual scenario is to resolve an issue between two parties. The most usual of these is to determine which of the two parties is to choose whether to take the first move, or otherwise determine the starting arrangement, in a game or sport.
A coin is thrown into the air in such a manner that it turns over and over. This can be conveniently achieved by coiling the hand into a loose fist, balancing the coin on the first knuckle of the index finger, and then pinging the thumb up from under the index finger by a physical process derived from the science of catastrophe theory. The nail of the thumb will then impact smartly against the edge of the coin which projects over the edge of the index finger's knuckle, propelling it upwards in such a manner that, by an application of the physics of angular momentum, it will be caused to rotate more-or-less rapidly about a diameter.
When it descends, the side which will remain uppermost will be dependent upon both the speed of rotation and the distance travelled, neither of which is easy to gauge precisely enough to make the process predictable.
The descending coin will either be caught in the hand by the one flipping it (the "tosser"), to be immediately clasped to the back of the other hand, or allowed to fall directly to the ground. The other party (the "caller") is offered the chance of guessing which of the two sides of the coin is on top. The two sides are usually referred to (in the English-speaking world) as "heads" and "tails". The "heads" side tends to be the one that features the head of state of the nation to which the coin belongs, while the "tails" is the other side.
Once the caller has made the call, the uppermost side of the coin is revealed. If the side matches that called by the caller, the caller has won, and is given the option of taking the choice as to the starting configuration of the game. Otherwise the tosser has won, and takes the option.<ref>The play ''{{WP|Rosencrantz_and_Guildenstern_Are_Dead|Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are Dead}}'' by {{WP|Tom_Stoppard|Tom Stoppard}} begins with an unfeasibly long sequence of "heads" calls in a game of '''coin-tossing'''.</ref>
\end{definition}

[Document 5]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Markov Chain]
Let $\sequence {X_n}_{n \mathop \ge 0}$ be a stochastic process over a countable set $S$.
Let $\map \Pr X$ denote the probability of the random variable $X$.
Let $\sequence {X_n}_{n \mathop \ge 0}$ satisfy the Markov property:
:$\condprob {X_{n + 1} = i_{n + 1} } {X_0 = i_0, X_1 = i_1, \ldots, X_n = i_n} = \condprob {X_{n + 1} = i_{n + 1} } {X_n = i_n}$
for all $n \ge 0$ and all $i_0, i_1, \ldots, i_{n + 1} \in S$.
That is, such that the conditional probability of $X_{i + 1}$ is dependent only upon $X_i$ and upon no earlier values of $\sequence {X_n}$.
That is, the state of $\sequence {X_n}$ in the future is unaffected by its history.
Then $\sequence {X_n}_{n \mathop \ge 0}$ is a '''Markov chain'''.
\end{definition}

The transition probabilities are such that the probability of staying in the same state (either eruption to eruption or normal to normal) is 0.55. Therefore, the probability of switching states is 1 - 0.55 = 0.45. 

The sensor sends alerts with certain probabilities: if it's an eruption day, there's a 60% chance of an alert (so P(alert | eruption) = 0.6 and P(no alert | eruption) = 0.4). On a normal day, the sensor has a 40% chance of alerting, so P(alert | normal) = 0.4 and P(no alert | normal) = 0.6.

The observed signals over three days are: alert (day 1), no alert (day 2), alert (day 3). We need to find the most probable sequence of states (eruption or normal) that could have generated this observation.

This sounds like a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) problem where we need to find the most likely state sequence given the observations. The standard approach for this is the Viterbi algorithm. Let me try to apply that step by step.

First, let's define the states as E (eruption, 1) and N (normal, 0). The observations are O1: alert, O2: no alert, O3: alert.

The Viterbi algorithm works by keeping track of the maximum probability of being in a state at time t given the observations up to t, along with the path that leads to it.

Let me outline the steps for three days.

Day 1:

We calculate the probability for each state (E or N) given the first observation (alert).

For E on day 1:
Initial probability of E is 0.6. The probability of observing an alert given E is 0.6. So the joint probability is 0.6 * 0.6 = 0.36.

For N on day 1:
Initial probability of N is 0.4. The probability of observing an alert given N is 0.4. So 0.4 * 0.4 = 0.16.

So the max probabilities for day 1 are:
E: 0.36
N: 0.16

The best paths so far are:
E: [E]
N: [N]

Day 2:

Now, for each state on day 2 (E or N), we consider the possible previous states (E or N from day 1) and compute the probabilities.

For day 2, the observation is no alert.

Let's compute for day 2 being E:

To get to E on day 2, it could have come from E on day 1 (with transition probability 0.55) or from N on day 1 (with transition probability 0.45).

From E to E:
Previous max probability for E on day 1 is 0.36. Multiply by transition 0.55. Then multiply by the observation probability for E and no alert. Since the observation is no alert, P(no alert | E) is 0.4. So total is 0.36 * 0.55 * 0.4.

The maximum between these is 0.0792, so the best path to E on day 2 is via E on day 1. The probability is 0.0792.

The most likely sequence of environmental conditions over the three days, given the observed alert signals, is determined using the Viterbi algorithm for Hidden Markov Models. Here's the step-by-step breakdown:

1. **States and Observations**:
   - **States**: Eruption (1) and Normal (0).
   - **Observations**: [Alert, No Alert, Alert].

2. **Transition Probabilities**:
   - P(Eruption → Eruption) = 0.55, P(Eruption → Normal) = 0.45.
   - P(Normal → Normal) = 0.55, P(Normal → Eruption) = 0.45.

3. **Emission Probabilities**:
   - P(Alert | Eruption) = 0.6, P(No Alert | Eruption) = 0.4.
   - P(Alert | Normal) = 0.4, P(No Alert | Normal) = 0.6.

4. **Initial Probabilities**:
   - P(Eruption) = 0.6, P(Normal) = 0.4.

5. **Viterbi Algorithm**:
   - **Day 1**:
     - Eruption: $0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.36$.
     - Normal: $0.4 \times 0.4 = 0.16$.
   - **Day 2** (Observation: No Alert):
     - Eruption: $\max(0.36 \times 0.55 \times 0.4, 0.16 \times 0.45 \times 0.4) = 0.0792$.
     - Normal: $\max(0.36 \times 0.45 \times 0.6, 0.16 \times 0.55 \times 0.6) = 0.0972$.
   - **Day 3** (Observation: Alert):
     - Eruption: $\max(0.0792 \times 0.55 \times 0.6, 0.0972 \times 0.45 \times 0.6) = 0.026244$.
     - Normal: $\max(0.0792 \times 0.45 \times 0.4, 0.0972 \times 0.55 \times 0.4) = 0.021384$.

6. **Most Likely Path**:
   - The sequence **Eruption → Normal → Eruption** ([1, 0, 1]) has the highest probability ($0.026244$).

**Answer**:  
$$
\boxed{[1, 0, 1]}
$$

nodes.text: Of these, one or more will have a maximum order.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Select one of these and call it $G_1$.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Again, either $G_1$ has a proper non-trivial normal subgroup or it does not.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: If not, then:

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
:$\set e \lhd G_1 \lhd G$
is a composition series for $G$.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: By the Jordan-Hölder Theorem, there can be no other composition series which is longer.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: As $G_1$ is a proper subgroup of $G$:

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
:$\order {G_1} < \order G$
where $\order G$ denotes the order of $G$.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Again, if $G_1$ has one or more proper non-trivial normal subgroup, one or more will have a maximum order.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Select one of these and call it $G_2$.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Thus we form a normal series:

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: :$\set e \lhd G_2 \lhd G_1 \lhd G$

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
The process can be repeated, and at each stage a normal subgroup is added to the series of a smaller 
order than the previous one.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: This process can not continue infinitely.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Eventually a $G_n$ will be encountered which has no proper non-trivial normal subgroup.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Thus a composition series:

nodes.label: context
context
nodes.text:
[Document 2]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Valid Time Indication]
Consider an analogue clock with an hour hand $H$ and a minute hand $M$.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: A '''valid time indication''' is an arrangement of $H$ and $M$ which indicates a specific time of day (either a.m. or p.m.).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: {{handwaving|It is not at all clear what it means to "indicate a specific time of day" since we have not defined how a clock works. I can imagine we don't want to go that far because it would be beside the point, but then we should explain that it is different from a random orientation of H and M which does not correspond to normal operation of a clock.}}

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
Category:Definitions/Clocks
\end{definition}

nodes.label: context
context
nodes.text:
\begin{proof}
The day of the week on which the $13$th falls is directy dependent upon the day of the week that the $1$st of the month falls.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: From Months that Start on the Same Day of the Week, the months can be grouped into equivalence classes according to which day the month starts:

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: For a non-leap year, the set of equivalence classes is:

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: :$\set {\set {\text {January}, \text {October} }, \set {\text {February}, \text {March}, \text {November} }, \set {\text {April}, \text {July} }, \set {\text {May} }, \set {\text {June} }, \set {\text {August} }, \set {\text {September}, \text {December} } }$

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: For a leap year, the set of equivalence classes is:

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: :$\set {\set {\text {January}, \text {April}, \text {July} }, \set {\text {February}, \text {August} }, \set {\text {March}, \text {November} }, \set {\text {May} }, \set {\text {June} }, \set {\text {September}, \text {December} }, \set {\text {October} } }$

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: It can be seen that none of these equivalence classes has fewer than $1$ day, and none has more than $3$.

nodes.label: context
context
nodes.text:
[Document 4]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Coin/Coin-Tossing]
'''Coin-tossing''' is a technique to select randomly one of two options.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The usual scenario is to resolve an issue between two parties.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The most usual of these is to determine which of the two parties is to choose whether to take the first move, or otherwise determine the starting arrangement, in a game or sport.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: A coin is thrown into the air in such a manner that it turns over and over.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: This can be conveniently achieved by coiling the hand into a loose fist, balancing the coin on the first knuckle of the index finger, and then pinging the thumb up from under the index finger by a physical process derived from the science of catastrophe theory.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The nail of the thumb will then impact smartly against the edge of the coin which projects over the edge of the index finger's knuckle, propelling it upwards in such a manner that, by an application of the physics of angular momentum, it will be caused to rotate more-or-less rapidly about a diameter.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: When it descends, the side which will remain uppermost will be dependent upon both the speed of rotation and the distance travelled, neither of which is easy to gauge precisely enough to make the process predictable.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The descending coin will either be caught in the hand by the one flipping it (the "tosser"), to be immediately clasped to the back of the other hand, or allowed to fall directly to the ground.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The other party (the "caller") is offered the chance of guessing which of the two sides of the coin is on top.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The two sides are usually referred to (in the English-speaking world) as "heads" and "tails".

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The "heads" side tends to be the one that features the head of state of the nation to which the coin belongs, while the "tails" is the other side.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Once the caller has made the call, the uppermost side of the coin is revealed.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: If the side matches that called by the caller, the caller has won, and is given the option of taking the choice as to the starting configuration of the game.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
Otherwise the tosser has won, and takes the option.<ref>The play ''{{WP|Rosencrantz_and_Guildenstern_Are_Dead|Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are Dead}}'' by {{WP|Tom_Stoppard|Tom Stoppard}} begins with an unfeasibly long sequence of "heads" calls in a game of '''coin-tossing'''.</ref>
\end{definition}

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
[Document 5]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Markov Chain]
Let $\sequence {X_n}_{n \mathop \ge 0}$ be a stochastic process over a countable set $S$.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Let $\map \Pr X$ denote the probability of the random variable $X$.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Let $\sequence {X_n}_{n \mathop \ge 0}$ satisfy the Markov property:

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
:$\condprob {X_{n + 1} = i_{n + 1} } {X_0 = i_0, X_1 = i_1, \ldots, X_n = i_n} = \condprob {X_{n + 1} = i_{n + 1} } {X_n = i_n}$
for all $n \ge 0$ and all $i_0, i_1, \ldots, i_{n + 1} \in S$.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: That is, such that the conditional probability of $X_{i + 1}$ is dependent only upon $X_i$ and upon no earlier values of $\sequence {X_n}$.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: That is, the state of $\sequence {X_n}$ in the future is unaffected by its history.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
Then $\sequence {X_n}_{n \mathop \ge 0}$ is a '''Markov chain'''.
\end{definition}

nodes.label: reasoning
nodes.text: The most likely sequence of environmental conditions over the three days, given the observed alert signals, is determined using the Viterbi algorithm for Hidden Markov Models.

nodes.label: planning
nodes.text: Here's the step-by-step breakdown:

nodes.label: restatement
nodes.text:
1. **States and Observations**:
   - **States**: Eruption (1) and Normal (0).
   - **Observations**: [Alert, No Alert, Alert].

nodes.label: restatement
nodes.text:
2. **Transition Probabilities**:
   - P(Eruption → Eruption) = 0.55, P(Eruption → Normal) = 0.45.
   - P(Normal → Normal) = 0.55, P(Normal → Eruption) = 0.45.

nodes.label: restatement
nodes.text:
3. **Emission Probabilities**:
   - P(Alert | Eruption) = 0.6, P(No Alert | Eruption) = 0.4.
   - P(Alert | Normal) = 0.4, P(No Alert | Normal) = 0.6.

nodes.label: restatement
nodes.text:
4. **Initial Probabilities**:
   - P(Eruption) = 0.6, P(Normal) = 0.4.

nodes.label: planning
nodes.text: 5. **Viterbi Algorithm**:

nodes.label: reasoning
